reasoning: The Lean formalization correctly encodes all components of the informal statement. "Infinitely many positive integers n" is expressed via the standard unboundedness idiom `∀ n:ℕ, ∃ m:ℕ, m > n ∧ P m`, which guarantees infinitely many m, all positive (since m > n ≥ 0 implies m ≥ 1). The inner predicate P correctly captures "all primes p dividing n²+3 also divide k²+3 for some k with k²<n". The informal says "some integer k" while Lean uses k:ℕ, but since k²=(−k)² for any integer, and both the divisibility condition p ∣ k²+3 and the inequality k²<n depend only on k², quantifying over ℕ versus ℤ yields equivalent statements. The types and divisibility encoding (casting to ℤ for the divisibility relation) are standard and semantically equivalent.
